His base of operations was in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja||ja|下野国}}, specifically in the {{ill|Kawachi District|ja||ja|河内郡}}. He is believed to have founded {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja||ja|下野薬師寺跡}}, which became the clan's temple.
== Career == In the third year of [[Empress Jitō]]’s reign (689), Komaro successfully petitioned to free 600 [[slaves]], an action approved by the court. At the time, he held the rank of {{ill|Chokushishi|ja|直広肆}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Empress Jitō, 3rd year, 10th month, 22nd day.</ref>
In the fourth year of [[Emperor Monmu]]'s reign (700), Komaro was appointed, along with {{ill|Prince Osakabe|ja|忍壁皇子}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|ja|藤原不比等}}, and {{ill|Awata no Mahito|ja|粟田真人}}, to draft the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}. At the time, he held the rank of {{ill|Chokushisan|ja|直広参}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Emperor Monmu, 4th year, 6th month, 17th day.</ref> The following year (701), after the enforcement of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, he was promoted to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下}}. In April of the same year, Komaro and two others lectured on the new laws to the court officials.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 4th month, 7th day.</ref> By August, the Taihō Code was completed.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 8th month, 3rd day.</ref> In recognition of his contributions, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} in 702 and was later granted 30 ''kōden'' (fields for merit) and 50 ''fuko'' (households for support).<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 3rd year, 2nd month, 15th day and 3rd month, 7th day.</ref>
Under Emperor Monmu, Komaro served as {{ill|Hyōbu-kyō|ja|兵部卿}} while being promoted to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従四位上}} in 705.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin,'' Monmu 4th year, 4th month, 20th day.</ref> In 707, following the emperor’s death, Komaro was appointed to oversee the construction of the imperial mausoleum.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Keiun 4th year, 10th month, 3rd day.</ref> Additionally, he requested a name change for his relative {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}}, granting permission to adopt the surname {{ill|Shimotsukeno Kawachi|ja|下毛野川内朝臣}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Keiun 4th year, 3rd month, 22nd day.</ref>
During the [[Empress Genmei]]'s reign in 708, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}} and later {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}}. However, he passed away in December 709.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Wado 2nd year, 12th month, 20th day.</ref> Following his death, there were no Sangi appointments for eight years until {{ill|Fujiwara no Fusasaki|ja|ja|藤原房前}} assumed the role in 717.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin.''</ref>
== Legacy == In 2016, the history of Shimotsukeno no Komaro was adapted into a manga by the Oyamamachi North {{ill|Rotary Club|ja|ロータリークラブ}}.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, April 14, 2016.</ref> In 2017, the manga was converted into a video format.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, February 19, 2017.</ref>
== Official Titles == According to the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史}}: * [[Empress Jitō]], 3rd year (689): {{ill|Chokushishi|ja|直広肆}} * [[Emperor Monmu]], 4th year (700): {{ill|Chokushisan|ja|直広参}} * [[Taihō]], 1st year (701): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下}}, Right {{ill|Ōben|ja|大弁}} * Taihō, 2nd year (702): {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} * Taihō, 3rd year (703): Merit fields (10 ''kōden'' and 20 ''kōden'') * [[Keiun]], 2nd year (705): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従四位上}}, concurrently appointed {{ill|Hyōbu-kyō|ja|兵部卿}} * Keiun, 4th year (707): Overseer of the Imperial Mausoleum * [[Wadō]], 1st year (708): {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}}, {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}} * Wadō, 2nd year (709): Passed away (Sangi, Shikibu-kyō, Taishōgun)

'''Shimotsukeno no Komaro''' (下毛野 古麻呂) was a noble of the late [[Asuka period]]. His name is also recorded as '''Komaro''' (子麻呂). His [[kabane]] was initially ''Kimi'' and later ''Asomi''. He was the son of {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Kusimaro|ja|下毛野久志麻呂}}, who held the rank of {{ill|Daigi|ja|大義 (冠位)}}. Komaro’s final rank was {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|ja|正四位下}}, and he served as {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}.
== Origins == Shimotsukeno no Komaro belonged to the {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|ja|下毛野氏}}, which originated from the {{ill|Shimotsukeno Kokuzō||ja|下毛野国造}} lineage. His base of operations was in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja||ja|下野国}}, specifically in the {{ill|Kawachi District|ja||ja|河内郡}}. He is believed to have founded {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja||ja|下野薬師寺跡}}, which became the clan's temple.

At the time, he held the rank of {{ill|Chokushisan|ja|直広参}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Emperor Monmu, 4th year, 6th month, 17th day.</ref> The following year (701), after the enforcement of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, he was promoted to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下}}. In April of the same year, Komaro and two others lectured on the new laws to the court officials.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 4th month, 7th day.</ref> By August, the Taihō Code was completed.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 8th month, 3rd day.</ref> In recognition of his contributions, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} in 702 and was later granted 30 ''kōden'' (fields for merit) and 50 ''fuko'' (households for support).<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 3rd year, 2nd month, 15th day and 3rd month, 7th day.</ref>

During the [[Empress Genmei]]'s reign in 708, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}} and later {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}}. However, he passed away in December 709.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Wado 2nd year, 12th month, 20th day.</ref> Following his death, there were no Sangi appointments for eight years until {{ill|Fujiwara no Fusasaki|ja|ja|藤原房前}} assumed the role in 717.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin.''</ref>

Under Emperor Monmu, Komaro served as {{ill|Hyōbu-kyō|ja|兵部卿}} while being promoted to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従四位上}} in 705.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin,'' Monmu 4th year, 4th month, 20th day.</ref> In 707, following the emperor’s death, Komaro was appointed to oversee the construction of the imperial mausoleum.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Keiun 4th year, 10th month, 3rd day.</ref> Additionally, he requested a name change for his relative {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}}, granting permission to adopt the surname {{ill|Shimotsukeno Kawachi|ja|下毛野川内朝臣}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Keiun 4th year, 3rd month, 22nd day.</ref>
During the [[Empress Genmei]]'s reign in 708, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}} and later {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}}. However, he passed away in December 709.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Wado 2nd year, 12th month, 20th day.</ref> Following his death, there were no Sangi appointments for eight years until {{ill|Fujiwara no Fusasaki|ja|ja|藤原房前}} assumed the role in 717.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin.''</ref>
== Legacy == In 2016, the history of Shimotsukeno no Komaro was adapted into a manga by the Oyamamachi North {{ill|Rotary Club|ja|ロータリークラブ}}.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, April 14, 2016.</ref> In 2017, the manga was converted into a video format.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, February 19, 2017.</ref>
== Official Titles == According to the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史}}: * [[Empress Jitō]], 3rd year (689): {{ill|Chokushishi|ja|直広肆}} * [[Emperor Monmu]], 4th year (700): {{ill|Chokushisan|ja|直広参}} * [[Taihō]], 1st year (701): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下}}, Right {{ill|Ōben|ja|大弁}} * Taihō, 2nd year (702): {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} * Taihō, 3rd year (703): Merit fields (10 ''kōden'' and 20 ''kōden'') * [[Keiun]], 2nd year (705): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従四位上}}, concurrently appointed {{ill|Hyōbu-kyō|ja|兵部卿}} * Keiun, 4th year (707): Overseer of the Imperial Mausoleum * [[Wadō]], 1st year (708): {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}}, {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}} * Wadō, 2nd year (709): Passed away (Sangi, Shikibu-kyō, Taishōgun)

'''Shimotsukeno no Komaro''' (下毛野 古麻呂) was a noble of the late [[Asuka period]]. His name is also recorded as '''Komaro''' (子麻呂). His [[kabane]] was initially ''Kimi'' and later ''Asomi''. He was the son of {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Kusimaro|ja|下毛野久志麻呂}}, who held the rank of {{ill|Daigi|ja|大義 (冠位)}}. Komaro’s final rank was {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|ja|正四位下}}, and he served as {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}.
== Origins == Shimotsukeno no Komaro belonged to the {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|ja|下毛野氏}}, which originated from the {{ill|Shimotsukeno Kokuzō||ja|下毛野国造}} lineage. His base of operations was in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja||ja|下野国}}, specifically in the {{ill|Kawachi District|ja||ja|河内郡}}. He is believed to have founded {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja||ja|下野薬師寺跡}}, which became the clan's temple.
== Career == In the third year of [[Empress Jitō]]’s reign (689), Komaro successfully petitioned to free 600 [[slaves]], an action approved by the court. At the time, he held the rank of {{ill|Chokushishi|ja|直広肆}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Empress Jitō, 3rd year, 10th month, 22nd day.</ref>
In the fourth year of [[Emperor Monmu]]'s reign (700), Komaro was appointed, along with {{ill|Prince Osakabe|ja|忍壁皇子}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|ja|藤原不比等}}, and {{ill|Awata no Mahito|ja|粟田真人}}, to draft the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}. At the time, he held the rank of {{ill|Chokushisan|ja|直広参}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Emperor Monmu, 4th year, 6th month, 17th day.</ref> The following year (701), after the enforcement of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, he was promoted to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下}}. In April of the same year, Komaro and two others lectured on the new laws to the court officials.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 4th month, 7th day.</ref> By August, the Taihō Code was completed.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 8th month, 3rd day.</ref> In recognition of his contributions, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} in 702 and was later granted 30 ''kōden'' (fields for merit) and 50 ''fuko'' (households for support).<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 3rd year, 2nd month, 15th day and 3rd month, 7th day.</ref>
Under Emperor Monmu, Komaro served as {{ill|Hyōbu-kyō|ja|兵部卿}} while being promoted to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従四位上}} in 705.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin,'' Monmu 4th year, 4th month, 20th day.</ref> In 707, following the emperor’s death, Komaro was appointed to oversee the construction of the imperial mausoleum.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Keiun 4th year, 10th month, 3rd day.</ref> Additionally, he requested a name change for his relative {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}}, granting permission to adopt the surname {{ill|Shimotsukeno Kawachi|ja|下毛野川内朝臣}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Keiun 4th year, 3rd month, 22nd day.</ref>
During the [[Empress Genmei]]'s reign in 708, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}} and later {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}}. However, he passed away in December 709.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Wado 2nd year, 12th month, 20th day.</ref> Following his death, there were no Sangi appointments for eight years until {{ill|Fujiwara no Fusasaki|ja|ja|藤原房前}} assumed the role in 717.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin.''</ref>
== Legacy == In 2016, the history of Shimotsukeno no Komaro was adapted into a manga by the Oyamamachi North {{ill|Rotary Club|ja|ロータリークラブ}}.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, April 14, 2016.</ref> In 2017, the manga was converted into a video format.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, February 19, 2017.</ref>
== Official Titles == According to the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史}}: * [[Empress Jitō]], 3rd year (689): {{ill|Chokushishi|ja|直広肆}} * [[Emperor Monmu]], 4th year (700): {{ill|Chokushisan|ja|直広参}} * [[Taihō]], 1st year (701): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下}}, Right {{ill|Ōben|ja|大弁}} * Taihō, 2nd year (702): {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} * Taihō, 3rd year (703): Merit fields (10 ''kōden'' and 20 ''kōden'') * [[Keiun]], 2nd year (705): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従四位上}}, concurrently appointed {{ill|Hyōbu-kyō|ja|兵部卿}} * Keiun, 4th year (707): Overseer of the Imperial Mausoleum * [[Wadō]], 1st year (708): {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}}, {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}} * Wadō, 2nd year (709): Passed away (Sangi, Shikibu-kyō, Taishōgun)
== References == * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟}}, ''Shoku Nihongi (Vol. 1),'' [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1992, ISBN 4061590308. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎}}, supervised by {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄}}, ''Dictionary of Japanese Ancient Clan Names,'' [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]], 1990, ISBN 4642022430. * {{ill|Takeuchi Risan|ja|竹内理三}}, ''Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Names (Vol. 4),'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1963, ISBN 4642020047. * [https://www.bgf.or.jp/bgmanga/?s=%E4%B8%8B%E6%AF%9B%E9%87%8E%E5%8F%A4%E9%BA%BB%E5%91%82 Free Manga: "Shimotsukeno no Komaro"], Published by the Shimotsuke City Board of Education, March 2022.

However, he passed away in December 709.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Wado 2nd year, 12th month, 20th day.</ref> Following his death, there were no Sangi appointments for eight years until {{ill|Fujiwara no Fusasaki|ja|ja|藤原房前}} assumed the role in 717.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin.''</ref>
== Legacy == In 2016, the history of Shimotsukeno no Komaro was adapted into a manga by the Oyamamachi North {{ill|Rotary Club|ja|ロータリークラブ}}.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, April 14, 2016.</ref> In 2017, the manga was converted into a video format.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, February 19, 2017.</ref>
== Official Titles == According to the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史}}: * [[Empress Jitō]], 3rd year (689): {{ill|Chokushishi|ja|直広肆}} * [[Emperor Monmu]], 4th year (700): {{ill|Chokushisan|ja|直広参}} * [[Taihō]], 1st year (701): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下}}, Right {{ill|Ōben|ja|大弁}} * Taihō, 2nd year (702): {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} * Taihō, 3rd year (703): Merit fields (10 ''kōden'' and 20 ''kōden'') * [[Keiun]], 2nd year (705): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従四位上}}, concurrently appointed {{ill|Hyōbu-kyō|ja|兵部卿}} * Keiun, 4th year (707): Overseer of the Imperial Mausoleum * [[Wadō]], 1st year (708): {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}}, {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}} * Wadō, 2nd year (709): Passed away (Sangi, Shikibu-kyō, Taishōgun)
== References == * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟}}, ''Shoku Nihongi (Vol. 1),'' [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1992, ISBN 4061590308. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎}}, supervised by {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄}}, ''Dictionary of Japanese Ancient Clan Names,'' [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]], 1990, ISBN 4642022430. * {{ill|Takeuchi Risan|ja|竹内理三}}, ''Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Names (Vol. 4),'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1963, ISBN 4642020047. * [https://www.bgf.or.jp/bgmanga/?s=%E4%B8%8B%E6%AF%9B%E9%87%8E%E5%8F%A4%E9%BA%BB%E5%91%82 Free Manga: "Shimotsukeno no Komaro"], Published by the Shimotsuke City Board of Education, March 2022.

'''Shimotsukeno no Komaro''' (下毛野 古麻呂) was a noble of the late [[Asuka period]]. His name is also recorded as '''Komaro''' (子麻呂). His [[kabane]] was initially ''Kimi'' and later ''Asomi''. He was the son of {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Kusimaro|ja|下毛野久志麻呂}}, who held the rank of {{ill|Daigi|ja|大義 (冠位)}}. Komaro’s final rank was {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|ja|正四位下}}, and he served as {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}.
== Origins == Shimotsukeno no Komaro belonged to the {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|ja|下毛野氏}}, which originated from the {{ill|Shimotsukeno Kokuzō||ja|下毛野国造}} lineage. His base of operations was in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja||ja|下野国}}, specifically in the {{ill|Kawachi District|ja||ja|河内郡}}. He is believed to have founded {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja||ja|下野薬師寺跡}}, which became the clan's temple.
== Career == In the third year of [[Empress Jitō]]’s reign (689), Komaro successfully petitioned to free 600 [[slaves]], an action approved by the court. At the time, he held the rank of {{ill|Chokushishi|ja|直広肆}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Empress Jitō, 3rd year, 10th month, 22nd day.</ref>
In the fourth year of [[Emperor Monmu]]'s reign (700), Komaro was appointed, along with {{ill|Prince Osakabe|ja|忍壁皇子}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|ja|藤原不比等}}, and {{ill|Awata no Mahito|ja|粟田真人}}, to draft the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}. At the time, he held the rank of {{ill|Chokushisan|ja|直広参}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Emperor Monmu, 4th year, 6th month, 17th day.</ref> The following year (701), after the enforcement of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, he was promoted to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下}}. In April of the same year, Komaro and two others lectured on the new laws to the court officials.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 4th month, 7th day.</ref> By August, the Taihō Code was completed.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 8th month, 3rd day.</ref> In recognition of his contributions, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} in 702 and was later granted 30 ''kōden'' (fields for merit) and 50 ''fuko'' (households for support).<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 3rd year, 2nd month, 15th day and 3rd month, 7th day.</ref>
Under Emperor Monmu, Komaro served as {{ill|Hyōbu-kyō|ja|兵部卿}} while being promoted to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従四位上}} in 705.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin,'' Monmu 4th year, 4th month, 20th day.</ref> In 707, following the emperor’s death, Komaro was appointed to oversee the construction of the imperial mausoleum.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Keiun 4th year, 10th month, 3rd day.</ref> Additionally, he requested a name change for his relative {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}}, granting permission to adopt the surname {{ill|Shimotsukeno Kawachi|ja|下毛野川内朝臣}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Keiun 4th year, 3rd month, 22nd day.</ref>
During the [[Empress Genmei]]'s reign in 708, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}} and later {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}}. However, he passed away in December 709.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Wado 2nd year, 12th month, 20th day.</ref> Following his death, there were no Sangi appointments for eight years until {{ill|Fujiwara no Fusasaki|ja|ja|藤原房前}} assumed the role in 717.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin.''</ref>
== Legacy == In 2016, the history of Shimotsukeno no Komaro was adapted into a manga by the Oyamamachi North {{ill|Rotary Club|ja|ロータリークラブ}}.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, April 14, 2016.</ref> In 2017, the manga was converted into a video format.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, February 19, 2017.</ref>
== Official Titles == According to the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史}}: * [[Empress Jitō]], 3rd year (689): {{ill|Chokushishi|ja|直広肆}} * [[Emperor Monmu]], 4th year (700): {{ill|Chokushisan|ja|直広参}} * [[Taihō]], 1st year (701): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下}}, Right {{ill|Ōben|ja|大弁}} * Taihō, 2nd year (702): {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} * Taihō, 3rd year (703): Merit fields (10 ''kōden'' and 20 ''kōden'') * [[Keiun]], 2nd year (705): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従四位上}}, concurrently appointed {{ill|Hyōbu-kyō|ja|兵部卿}} * Keiun, 4th year (707): Overseer of the Imperial Mausoleum * [[Wadō]], 1st year (708): {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}}, {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}} * Wadō, 2nd year (709): Passed away (Sangi, Shikibu-kyō, Taishōgun)
== References == * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟}}, ''Shoku Nihongi (Vol. 1),'' [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1992, ISBN 4061590308. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎}}, supervised by {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄}}, ''Dictionary of Japanese Ancient Clan Names,'' [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]], 1990, ISBN 4642022430. * {{ill|Takeuchi Risan|ja|竹内理三}}, ''Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Names (Vol. 4),'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1963, ISBN 4642020047. * [https://www.bgf.or.jp/bgmanga/?s=%E4%B8%8B%E6%AF%9B%E9%87%8E%E5%8F%A4%E9%BA%BB%E5%91%82 Free Manga: "Shimotsukeno no Komaro"], Published by the Shimotsuke City Board of Education, March 2022.

== References == * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟}}, ''Shoku Nihongi (Vol. 1),'' [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1992, ISBN 4061590308. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎}}, supervised by {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄}}, ''Dictionary of Japanese Ancient Clan Names,'' [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]], 1990, ISBN 4642022430. * {{ill|Takeuchi Risan|ja|竹内理三}}, ''Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Names (Vol. 4),'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1963, ISBN 4642020047. * [https://www.bgf.or.jp/bgmanga/?s=%E4%B8%8B%E6%AF%9B%E9%87%8E%E5%8F%A4%E9%BA%BB%E5%91%82 Free Manga: "Shimotsukeno no Komaro"], Published by the Shimotsuke City Board of Education, March 2022.

revision.text:
'''Shimotsukeno no Komaro''' (下毛野 古麻呂) was a noble of the late [[Asuka period]]. His name is also recorded as '''Komaro''' (子麻呂). His [[kabane]] was initially ''Kimi'' and later ''Asomi''. He was the son of {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Kusimaro|ja|下毛野久志麻呂}}, who held the rank of {{ill|Daigi|ja|大義 (冠位)}}. Komaro’s final rank was {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|ja|正四位下}}, and he served as {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}.
== Origins == Shimotsukeno no Komaro belonged to the {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|ja|下毛野氏}}, which originated from the {{ill|Shimotsukeno Kokuzō||ja|下毛野国造}} lineage. His base of operations was in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja||ja|下野国}}, specifically in the {{ill|Kawachi District|ja||ja|河内郡}}. He is believed to have founded {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja||ja|下野薬師寺跡}}, which became the clan's temple.
== Career == In the third year of [[Empress Jitō]]’s reign (689), Komaro successfully petitioned to free 600 [[slaves]], an action approved by the court. At the time, he held the rank of {{ill|Chokushishi|ja|直広肆}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Empress Jitō, 3rd year, 10th month, 22nd day.</ref>
In the fourth year of [[Emperor Monmu]]'s reign (700), Komaro was appointed, along with {{ill|Prince Osakabe|ja|忍壁皇子}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|ja|藤原不比等}}, and {{ill|Awata no Mahito|ja|粟田真人}}, to draft the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}. At the time, he held the rank of {{ill|Chokushisan|ja|直広参}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Emperor Monmu, 4th year, 6th month, 17th day.</ref> The following year (701), after the enforcement of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, he was promoted to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下}}. In April of the same year, Komaro and two others lectured on the new laws to the court officials.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 4th month, 7th day.</ref> By August, the Taihō Code was completed.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 8th month, 3rd day.</ref> In recognition of his contributions, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} in 702 and was later granted 30 ''kōden'' (fields for merit) and 50 ''fuko'' (households for support).<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 3rd year, 2nd month, 15th day and 3rd month, 7th day.</ref>
Under Emperor Monmu, Komaro served as {{ill|Hyōbu-kyō|ja|兵部卿}} while being promoted to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従四位上}} in 705.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin,'' Monmu 4th year, 4th month, 20th day.</ref> In 707, following the emperor’s death, Komaro was appointed to oversee the construction of the imperial mausoleum.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Keiun 4th year, 10th month, 3rd day.</ref> Additionally, he requested a name change for his relative {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}}, granting permission to adopt the surname {{ill|Shimotsukeno Kawachi|ja|下毛野川内朝臣}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Keiun 4th year, 3rd month, 22nd day.</ref>
During the [[Empress Genmei]]'s reign in 708, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}} and later {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}}. However, he passed away in December 709.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Wado 2nd year, 12th month, 20th day.</ref> Following his death, there were no Sangi appointments for eight years until {{ill|Fujiwara no Fusasaki|ja|ja|藤原房前}} assumed the role in 717.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin.''</ref>
== Legacy == In 2016, the history of Shimotsukeno no Komaro was adapted into a manga by the Oyamamachi North {{ill|Rotary Club|ja|ロータリークラブ}}.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, April 14, 2016.</ref> In 2017, the manga was converted into a video format.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, February 19, 2017.</ref>
== Official Titles == According to the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史}}: * [[Empress Jitō]], 3rd year (689): {{ill|Chokushishi|ja|直広肆}} * [[Emperor Monmu]], 4th year (700): {{ill|Chokushisan|ja|直広参}} * [[Taihō]], 1st year (701): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下}}, Right {{ill|Ōben|ja|大弁}} * Taihō, 2nd year (702): {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} * Taihō, 3rd year (703): Merit fields (10 ''kōden'' and 20 ''kōden'') * [[Keiun]], 2nd year (705): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従四位上}}, concurrently appointed {{ill|Hyōbu-kyō|ja|兵部卿}} * Keiun, 4th year (707): Overseer of the Imperial Mausoleum * [[Wadō]], 1st year (708): {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}}, {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}} * Wadō, 2nd year (709): Passed away (Sangi, Shikibu-kyō, Taishōgun)
== References == * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟}}, ''Shoku Nihongi (Vol. 1),'' [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1992, ISBN 4061590308. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎}}, supervised by {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄}}, ''Dictionary of Japanese Ancient Clan Names,'' [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]], 1990, ISBN 4642022430. * {{ill|Takeuchi Risan|ja|竹内理三}}, ''Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Names (Vol. 4),'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1963, ISBN 4642020047. * [https://www.bgf.or.jp/bgmanga/?s=%E4%B8%8B%E6%AF%9B%E9%87%8E%E5%8F%A4%E9%BA%BB%E5%91%82 Free Manga: "Shimotsukeno no Komaro"], Published by the Shimotsuke City Board of Education, March 2022.

revision.text:
'''Shimotsukeno no Komaro''' (下毛野 古麻呂) was a noble of the late [[Asuka period]]. His name is also recorded as '''Komaro''' (子麻呂). His [[kabane]] was initially ''Kimi'' and later ''Asomi''. He was the son of {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Kusimaro|ja|下毛野久志麻呂}}, who held the rank of {{ill|Daigi|ja|大義 (冠位)}}. Komaro’s final rank was {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|ja|正四位下}}, and he served as {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}.
== Origins == Shimotsukeno no Komaro belonged to the {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|ja|下毛野氏}}, which originated from the {{ill|Shimotsukeno Kokuzō||ja|下毛野国造}} lineage. His base of operations was in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja||ja|下野国}}, specifically in the {{ill|Kawachi District|ja||ja|河内郡}}. He is believed to have founded {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja||ja|下野薬師寺跡}}, which became the clan's temple.
== Career == In the third year of [[Empress Jitō]]’s reign (689), Komaro successfully petitioned to free 600 [[slaves]], an action approved by the court. At the time, he held the rank of {{ill|Chokushishi|ja|直広肆}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Empress Jitō, 3rd year, 10th month, 22nd day.</ref>
In the fourth year of [[Emperor Monmu]]'s reign (700), Komaro was appointed, along with {{ill|Prince Osakabe|ja|忍壁皇子}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|ja|藤原不比等}}, and {{ill|Awata no Mahito|ja|粟田真人}}, to draft the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}. At the time, he held the rank of {{ill|Chokushisan|ja|直広参}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Emperor Monmu, 4th year, 6th month, 17th day.</ref> The following year (701), after the enforcement of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, he was promoted to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下}}. In April of the same year, Komaro and two others lectured on the new laws to the court officials.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 4th month, 7th day.</ref> By August, the Taihō Code was completed.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 8th month, 3rd day.</ref> In recognition of his contributions, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} in 702 and was later granted 30 ''kōden'' (fields for merit) and 50 ''fuko'' (households for support).<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 3rd year, 2nd month, 15th day and 3rd month, 7th day.</ref>
Under Emperor Monmu, Komaro served as {{ill|Hyōbu-kyō|ja|兵部卿}} while being promoted to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従四位上}} in 705.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin,'' Monmu 4th year, 4th month, 20th day.</ref> In 707, following the emperor’s death, Komaro was appointed to oversee the construction of the imperial mausoleum.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Keiun 4th year, 10th month, 3rd day.</ref> Additionally, he requested a name change for his relative {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}}, granting permission to adopt the surname {{ill|Shimotsukeno Kawachi|ja|下毛野川内朝臣}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Keiun 4th year, 3rd month, 22nd day.</ref>
During the [[Empress Genmei]]'s reign in 708, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}} and later {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}}. However, he passed away in December 709.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Wado 2nd year, 12th month, 20th day.</ref> Following his death, there were no Sangi appointments for eight years until {{ill|Fujiwara no Fusasaki|ja|ja|藤原房前}} assumed the role in 717.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin.''</ref>
== Legacy == In 2016, the history of Shimotsukeno no Komaro was adapted into a manga by the Oyamamachi North {{ill|Rotary Club|ja|ロータリークラブ}}.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, April 14, 2016.</ref> In 2017, the manga was converted into a video format.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, February 19, 2017.</ref>
== Official Titles == According to the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史}}: * [[Empress Jitō]], 3rd year (689): {{ill|Chokushishi|ja|直広肆}} * [[Emperor Monmu]], 4th year (700): {{ill|Chokushisan|ja|直広参}} * [[Taihō]], 1st year (701): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下}}, Right {{ill|Ōben|ja|大弁}} * Taihō, 2nd year (702): {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} * Taihō, 3rd year (703): Merit fields (10 ''kōden'' and 20 ''kōden'') * [[Keiun]], 2nd year (705): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従四位上}}, concurrently appointed {{ill|Hyōbu-kyō|ja|兵部卿}} * Keiun, 4th year (707): Overseer of the Imperial Mausoleum * [[Wadō]], 1st year (708): {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}}, {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}} * Wadō, 2nd year (709): Passed away (Sangi, Shikibu-kyō, Taishōgun)
== References == * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟}}, ''Shoku Nihongi (Vol. 1),'' [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1992, ISBN 4061590308. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎}}, supervised by {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄}}, ''Dictionary of Japanese Ancient Clan Names,'' [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]], 1990, ISBN 4642022430. * {{ill|Takeuchi Risan|ja|竹内理三}}, ''Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Names (Vol. 4),'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1963, ISBN 4642020047. * [https://www.bgf.or.jp/bgmanga/?s=%E4%B8%8B%E6%AF%9B%E9%87%8E%E5%8F%A4%E9%BA%BB%E5%91%82 Free Manga: "Shimotsukeno no Komaro"], Published by the Shimotsuke City Board of Education, March 2022.

'''Shimotsukeno no Komaro''' (下毛野 古麻呂) was a noble of the late [[Asuka period]]. His name is also recorded as '''Komaro''' (子麻呂). His [[kabane]] was initially ''Kimi'' and later ''Asomi''. He was the son of {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Kusimaro|ja|下毛野久志麻呂}}, who held the rank of {{ill|Daigi|ja|大義 (冠位)}}. Komaro’s final rank was {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|ja|正四位下}}, and he served as {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}.
== Origins == Shimotsukeno no Komaro belonged to the {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|ja|下毛野氏}}, which originated from the {{ill|Shimotsukeno Kokuzō||ja|下毛野国造}} lineage. His base of operations was in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja||ja|下野国}}, specifically in the {{ill|Kawachi District|ja||ja|河内郡}}. He is believed to have founded {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja||ja|下野薬師寺跡}}, which became the clan's temple.
== Career == In the third year of [[Empress Jitō]]’s reign (689), Komaro successfully petitioned to free 600 [[slaves]], an action approved by the court. At the time, he held the rank of {{ill|Chokushishi|ja|直広肆}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Empress Jitō, 3rd year, 10th month, 22nd day.</ref>
In the fourth year of [[Emperor Monmu]]'s reign (700), Komaro was appointed, along with {{ill|Prince Osakabe|ja|忍壁皇子}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|ja|藤原不比等}}, and {{ill|Awata no Mahito|ja|粟田真人}}, to draft the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}. At the time, he held the rank of {{ill|Chokushisan|ja|直広参}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Emperor Monmu, 4th year, 6th month, 17th day.</ref> The following year (701), after the enforcement of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, he was promoted to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下}}. In April of the same year, Komaro and two others lectured on the new laws to the court officials.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 4th month, 7th day.</ref> By August, the Taihō Code was completed.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 8th month, 3rd day.</ref> In recognition of his contributions, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} in 702 and was later granted 30 ''kōden'' (fields for merit) and 50 ''fuko'' (households for support).<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 3rd year, 2nd month, 15th day and 3rd month, 7th day.</ref>

During the [[Empress Genmei]]'s reign in 708, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}} and later {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}}. However, he passed away in December 709.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Wado 2nd year, 12th month, 20th day.</ref> Following his death, there were no Sangi appointments for eight years until {{ill|Fujiwara no Fusasaki|ja|ja|藤原房前}} assumed the role in 717.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin.''</ref>
== Legacy == In 2016, the history of Shimotsukeno no Komaro was adapted into a manga by the Oyamamachi North {{ill|Rotary Club|ja|ロータリークラブ}}.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, April 14, 2016.</ref> In 2017, the manga was converted into a video format.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, February 19, 2017.</ref>
== Official Titles == According to the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史}}: * [[Empress Jitō]], 3rd year (689): {{ill|Chokushishi|ja|直広肆}} * [[Emperor Monmu]], 4th year (700): {{ill|Chokushisan|ja|直広参}} * [[Taihō]], 1st year (701): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下}}, Right {{ill|Ōben|ja|大弁}} * Taihō, 2nd year (702): {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} * Taihō, 3rd year (703): Merit fields (10 ''kōden'' and 20 ''kōden'') * [[Keiun]], 2nd year (705): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従四位上}}, concurrently appointed {{ill|Hyōbu-kyō|ja|兵部卿}} * Keiun, 4th year (707): Overseer of the Imperial Mausoleum * [[Wadō]], 1st year (708): {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}}, {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}} * Wadō, 2nd year (709): Passed away (Sangi, Shikibu-kyō, Taishōgun)
== References == * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟}}, ''Shoku Nihongi (Vol. 1),'' [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1992, ISBN 4061590308. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎}}, supervised by {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄}}, ''Dictionary of Japanese Ancient Clan Names,'' [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]], 1990, ISBN 4642022430. * {{ill|Takeuchi Risan|ja|竹内理三}}, ''Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Names (Vol. 4),'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1963, ISBN 4642020047. * [https://www.bgf.or.jp/bgmanga/?s=%E4%B8%8B%E6%AF%9B%E9%87%8E%E5%8F%A4%E9%BA%BB%E5%91%82 Free Manga: "Shimotsukeno no Komaro"], Published by the Shimotsuke City Board of Education, March 2022.

'''Shimotsukeno no Komaro''' (下毛野 古麻呂) was a noble of the late [[Asuka period]]. His name is also recorded as '''Komaro''' (子麻呂). His [[kabane]] was initially ''Kimi'' and later ''Asomi''. He was the son of {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Kusimaro|ja|下毛野久志麻呂}}, who held the rank of {{ill|Daigi|ja|大義 (冠位)}}. Komaro’s final rank was {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|ja|正四位下}}, and he served as {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}.
== Origins == Shimotsukeno no Komaro belonged to the {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|ja|下毛野氏}}, which originated from the {{ill|Shimotsukeno Kokuzō||ja|下毛野国造}} lineage. His base of operations was in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja||ja|下野国}}, specifically in the {{ill|Kawachi District|ja||ja|河内郡}}. He is believed to have founded {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja||ja|下野薬師寺跡}}, which became the clan's temple.
== Career == In the third year of [[Empress Jitō]]’s reign (689), Komaro successfully petitioned to free 600 [[slaves]], an action approved by the court. At the time, he held the rank of {{ill|Chokushishi|ja|直広肆}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Empress Jitō, 3rd year, 10th month, 22nd day.</ref>
In the fourth year of [[Emperor Monmu]]'s reign (700), Komaro was appointed, along with {{ill|Prince Osakabe|ja|忍壁皇子}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|ja|藤原不比等}}, and {{ill|Awata no Mahito|ja|粟田真人}}, to draft the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}. At the time, he held the rank of {{ill|Chokushisan|ja|直広参}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Emperor Monmu, 4th year, 6th month, 17th day.</ref> The following year (701), after the enforcement of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, he was promoted to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下}}. In April of the same year, Komaro and two others lectured on the new laws to the court officials.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 4th month, 7th day.</ref> By August, the Taihō Code was completed.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 8th month, 3rd day.</ref> In recognition of his contributions, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} in 702 and was later granted 30 ''kōden'' (fields for merit) and 50 ''fuko'' (households for support).<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 3rd year, 2nd month, 15th day and 3rd month, 7th day.</ref>
Under Emperor Monmu, Komaro served as {{ill|Hyōbu-kyō|ja|兵部卿}} while being promoted to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従四位上}} in 705.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin,'' Monmu 4th year, 4th month, 20th day.</ref> In 707, following the emperor’s death, Komaro was appointed to oversee the construction of the imperial mausoleum.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Keiun 4th year, 10th month, 3rd day.</ref> Additionally, he requested a name change for his relative {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}}, granting permission to adopt the surname {{ill|Shimotsukeno Kawachi|ja|下毛野川内朝臣}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Keiun 4th year, 3rd month, 22nd day.</ref>
During the [[Empress Genmei]]'s reign in 708, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}} and later {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}}. However, he passed away in December 709.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Wado 2nd year, 12th month, 20th day.</ref> Following his death, there were no Sangi appointments for eight years until {{ill|Fujiwara no Fusasaki|ja|ja|藤原房前}} assumed the role in 717.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin.''</ref>
== Legacy == In 2016, the history of Shimotsukeno no Komaro was adapted into a manga by the Oyamamachi North {{ill|Rotary Club|ja|ロータリークラブ}}.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, April 14, 2016.</ref> In 2017, the manga was converted into a video format.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, February 19, 2017.</ref>
== Official Titles == According to the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史}}: * [[Empress Jitō]], 3rd year (689): {{ill|Chokushishi|ja|直広肆}} * [[Emperor Monmu]], 4th year (700): {{ill|Chokushisan|ja|直広参}} * [[Taihō]], 1st year (701): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下}}, Right {{ill|Ōben|ja|大弁}} * Taihō, 2nd year (702): {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} * Taihō, 3rd year (703): Merit fields (10 ''kōden'' and 20 ''kōden'') * [[Keiun]], 2nd year (705): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従四位上}}, concurrently appointed {{ill|Hyōbu-kyō|ja|兵部卿}} * Keiun, 4th year (707): Overseer of the Imperial Mausoleum * [[Wadō]], 1st year (708): {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}}, {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}} * Wadō, 2nd year (709): Passed away (Sangi, Shikibu-kyō, Taishōgun)
== References == * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟}}, ''Shoku Nihongi (Vol. 1),'' [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1992, ISBN 4061590308. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎}}, supervised by {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄}}, ''Dictionary of Japanese Ancient Clan Names,'' [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]], 1990, ISBN 4642022430. * {{ill|Takeuchi Risan|ja|竹内理三}}, ''Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Names (Vol. 4),'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1963, ISBN 4642020047. * [https://www.bgf.or.jp/bgmanga/?s=%E4%B8%8B%E6%AF%9B%E9%87%8E%E5%8F%A4%E9%BA%BB%E5%91%82 Free Manga: "Shimotsukeno no Komaro"], Published by the Shimotsuke City Board of Education, March 2022.

'''Shimotsukeno no Komaro''' (下毛野 古麻呂) was a noble of the late [[Asuka period]]. His name is also recorded as '''Komaro''' (子麻呂). His [[kabane]] was initially ''Kimi'' and later ''Asomi''. He was the son of {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Kusimaro|ja|下毛野久志麻呂}}, who held the rank of {{ill|Daigi|ja|大義 (冠位)}}. Komaro’s final rank was {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|ja|正四位下}}, and he served as {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}.
== Origins == Shimotsukeno no Komaro belonged to the {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|ja|下毛野氏}}, which originated from the {{ill|Shimotsukeno Kokuzō||ja|下毛野国造}} lineage. His base of operations was in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja||ja|下野国}}, specifically in the {{ill|Kawachi District|ja||ja|河内郡}}. He is believed to have founded {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja||ja|下野薬師寺跡}}, which became the clan's temple.
== Career == In the third year of [[Empress Jitō]]’s reign (689), Komaro successfully petitioned to free 600 [[slaves]], an action approved by the court. At the time, he held the rank of {{ill|Chokushishi|ja|直広肆}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Empress Jitō, 3rd year, 10th month, 22nd day.</ref>
In the fourth year of [[Emperor Monmu]]'s reign (700), Komaro was appointed, along with {{ill|Prince Osakabe|ja|忍壁皇子}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|ja|藤原不比等}}, and {{ill|Awata no Mahito|ja|粟田真人}}, to draft the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}. At the time, he held the rank of {{ill|Chokushisan|ja|直広参}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Emperor Monmu, 4th year, 6th month, 17th day.</ref> The following year (701), after the enforcement of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, he was promoted to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下}}. In April of the same year, Komaro and two others lectured on the new laws to the court officials.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 4th month, 7th day.</ref> By August, the Taihō Code was completed.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 8th month, 3rd day.</ref> In recognition of his contributions, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} in 702 and was later granted 30 ''kōden'' (fields for merit) and 50 ''fuko'' (households for support).<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 3rd year, 2nd month, 15th day and 3rd month, 7th day.</ref>
Under Emperor Monmu, Komaro served as {{ill|Hyōbu-kyō|ja|兵部卿}} while being promoted to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従四位上}} in 705.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin,'' Monmu 4th year, 4th month, 20th day.</ref> In 707, following the emperor’s death, Komaro was appointed to oversee the construction of the imperial mausoleum.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Keiun 4th year, 10th month, 3rd day.</ref> Additionally, he requested a name change for his relative {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}}, granting permission to adopt the surname {{ill|Shimotsukeno Kawachi|ja|下毛野川内朝臣}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Keiun 4th year, 3rd month, 22nd day.</ref>
During the [[Empress Genmei]]'s reign in 708, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}} and later {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}}. However, he passed away in December 709.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Wado 2nd year, 12th month, 20th day.</ref> Following his death, there were no Sangi appointments for eight years until {{ill|Fujiwara no Fusasaki|ja|ja|藤原房前}} assumed the role in 717.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin.''</ref>
== Legacy == In 2016, the history of Shimotsukeno no Komaro was adapted into a manga by the Oyamamachi North {{ill|Rotary Club|ja|ロータリークラブ}}.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, April 14, 2016.</ref> In 2017, the manga was converted into a video format.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, February 19, 2017.</ref>

'''Shimotsukeno no Komaro''' (下毛野 古麻呂) was a noble of the late [[Asuka period]]. His name is also recorded as '''Komaro''' (子麻呂). His [[kabane]] was initially ''Kimi'' and later ''Asomi''. He was the son of {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Kusimaro|ja|下毛野久志麻呂}}, who held the rank of {{ill|Daigi|ja|大義 (冠位)}}. Komaro’s final rank was {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|ja|正四位下}}, and he served as {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}.
== Origins == Shimotsukeno no Komaro belonged to the {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|ja|下毛野氏}}, which originated from the {{ill|Shimotsukeno Kokuzō||ja|下毛野国造}} lineage. His base of operations was in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja||ja|下野国}}, specifically in the {{ill|Kawachi District|ja||ja|河内郡}}. He is believed to have founded {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja||ja|下野薬師寺跡}}, which became the clan's temple.
== Career == In the third year of [[Empress Jitō]]’s reign (689), Komaro successfully petitioned to free 600 [[slaves]], an action approved by the court. At the time, he held the rank of {{ill|Chokushishi|ja|直広肆}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Empress Jitō, 3rd year, 10th month, 22nd day.</ref>
In the fourth year of [[Emperor Monmu]]'s reign (700), Komaro was appointed, along with {{ill|Prince Osakabe|ja|忍壁皇子}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|ja|藤原不比等}}, and {{ill|Awata no Mahito|ja|粟田真人}}, to draft the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}. At the time, he held the rank of {{ill|Chokushisan|ja|直広参}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Emperor Monmu, 4th year, 6th month, 17th day.</ref> The following year (701), after the enforcement of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, he was promoted to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下}}. In April of the same year, Komaro and two others lectured on the new laws to the court officials.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 4th month, 7th day.</ref> By August, the Taihō Code was completed.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 8th month, 3rd day.</ref> In recognition of his contributions, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} in 702 and was later granted 30 ''kōden'' (fields for merit) and 50 ''fuko'' (households for support).<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 3rd year, 2nd month, 15th day and 3rd month, 7th day.</ref>
Under Emperor Monmu, Komaro served as {{ill|Hyōbu-kyō|ja|兵部卿}} while being promoted to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従四位上}} in 705.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin,'' Monmu 4th year, 4th month, 20th day.</ref> In 707, following the emperor’s death, Komaro was appointed to oversee the construction of the imperial mausoleum.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Keiun 4th year, 10th month, 3rd day.</ref> Additionally, he requested a name change for his relative {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}}, granting permission to adopt the surname {{ill|Shimotsukeno Kawachi|ja|下毛野川内朝臣}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Keiun 4th year, 3rd month, 22nd day.</ref>
During the [[Empress Genmei]]'s reign in 708, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}} and later {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}}. However, he passed away in December 709.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Wado 2nd year, 12th month, 20th day.</ref> Following his death, there were no Sangi appointments for eight years until {{ill|Fujiwara no Fusasaki|ja|ja|藤原房前}} assumed the role in 717.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin.''</ref>
== Legacy == In 2016, the history of Shimotsukeno no Komaro was adapted into a manga by the Oyamamachi North {{ill|Rotary Club|ja|ロータリークラブ}}.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, April 14, 2016.</ref> In 2017, the manga was converted into a video format.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, February 19, 2017.</ref>
== Official Titles == According to the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史}}: * [[Empress Jitō]], 3rd year (689): {{ill|Chokushishi|ja|直広肆}} * [[Emperor Monmu]], 4th year (700): {{ill|Chokushisan|ja|直広参}} * [[Taihō]], 1st year (701): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下}}, Right {{ill|Ōben|ja|大弁}} * Taihō, 2nd year (702): {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} * Taihō, 3rd year (703): Merit fields (10 ''kōden'' and 20 ''kōden'') * [[Keiun]], 2nd year (705): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従四位上}}, concurrently appointed {{ill|Hyōbu-kyō|ja|兵部卿}} * Keiun, 4th year (707): Overseer of the Imperial Mausoleum * [[Wadō]], 1st year (708): {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}}, {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}} * Wadō, 2nd year (709): Passed away (Sangi, Shikibu-kyō, Taishōgun)
== References == * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟}}, ''Shoku Nihongi (Vol. 1),'' [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1992, ISBN 4061590308. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎}}, supervised by {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄}}, ''Dictionary of Japanese Ancient Clan Names,'' [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]], 1990, ISBN 4642022430. * {{ill|Takeuchi Risan|ja|竹内理三}}, ''Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Names (Vol. 4),'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1963, ISBN 4642020047. * [https://www.bgf.or.jp/bgmanga/?s=%E4%B8%8B%E6%AF%9B%E9%87%8E%E5%8F%A4%E9%BA%BB%E5%91%82 Free Manga: "Shimotsukeno no Komaro"], Published by the Shimotsuke City Board of Education, March 2022.

'''Shimotsukeno no Komaro''' (下毛野 古麻呂) was a noble of the late [[Asuka period]]. His name is also recorded as '''Komaro''' (子麻呂). His [[kabane]] was initially ''Kimi'' and later ''Asomi''. He was the son of {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Kusimaro|ja|下毛野久志麻呂}}, who held the rank of {{ill|Daigi|ja|大義 (冠位)}}. Komaro’s final rank was {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|ja|正四位下}}, and he served as {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}.
== Origins == Shimotsukeno no Komaro belonged to the {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|ja|下毛野氏}}, which originated from the {{ill|Shimotsukeno Kokuzō||ja|下毛野国造}} lineage. His base of operations was in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja||ja|下野国}}, specifically in the {{ill|Kawachi District|ja||ja|河内郡}}. He is believed to have founded {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja||ja|下野薬師寺跡}}, which became the clan's temple.
== Career == In the third year of [[Empress Jitō]]’s reign (689), Komaro successfully petitioned to free 600 [[slaves]], an action approved by the court. At the time, he held the rank of {{ill|Chokushishi|ja|直広肆}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Empress Jitō, 3rd year, 10th month, 22nd day.</ref>
In the fourth year of [[Emperor Monmu]]'s reign (700), Komaro was appointed, along with {{ill|Prince Osakabe|ja|忍壁皇子}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|ja|藤原不比等}}, and {{ill|Awata no Mahito|ja|粟田真人}}, to draft the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}. At the time, he held the rank of {{ill|Chokushisan|ja|直広参}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Emperor Monmu, 4th year, 6th month, 17th day.</ref> The following year (701), after the enforcement of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, he was promoted to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下}}. In April of the same year, Komaro and two others lectured on the new laws to the court officials.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 4th month, 7th day.</ref> By August, the Taihō Code was completed.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 8th month, 3rd day.</ref> In recognition of his contributions, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} in 702 and was later granted 30 ''kōden'' (fields for merit) and 50 ''fuko'' (households for support).<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 3rd year, 2nd month, 15th day and 3rd month, 7th day.</ref>
Under Emperor Monmu, Komaro served as {{ill|Hyōbu-kyō|ja|兵部卿}} while being promoted to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従四位上}} in 705.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin,'' Monmu 4th year, 4th month, 20th day.</ref> In 707, following the emperor’s death, Komaro was appointed to oversee the construction of the imperial mausoleum.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Keiun 4th year, 10th month, 3rd day.</ref> Additionally, he requested a name change for his relative {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}}, granting permission to adopt the surname {{ill|Shimotsukeno Kawachi|ja|下毛野川内朝臣}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Keiun 4th year, 3rd month, 22nd day.</ref>
During the [[Empress Genmei]]'s reign in 708, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}} and later {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}}. However, he passed away in December 709.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Wado 2nd year, 12th month, 20th day.</ref> Following his death, there were no Sangi appointments for eight years until {{ill|Fujiwara no Fusasaki|ja|ja|藤原房前}} assumed the role in 717.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin.''</ref>
== Legacy == In 2016, the history of Shimotsukeno no Komaro was adapted into a manga by the Oyamamachi North {{ill|Rotary Club|ja|ロータリークラブ}}.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, April 14, 2016.</ref> In 2017, the manga was converted into a video format.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, February 19, 2017.</ref>
== Official Titles == According to the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史}}: * [[Empress Jitō]], 3rd year (689): {{ill|Chokushishi|ja|直広肆}} * [[Emperor Monmu]], 4th year (700): {{ill|Chokushisan|ja|直広参}} * [[Taihō]], 1st year (701): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下}}, Right {{ill|Ōben|ja|大弁}} * Taihō, 2nd year (702): {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} * Taihō, 3rd year (703): Merit fields (10 ''kōden'' and 20 ''kōden'') * [[Keiun]], 2nd year (705): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従四位上}}, concurrently appointed {{ill|Hyōbu-kyō|ja|兵部卿}} * Keiun, 4th year (707): Overseer of the Imperial Mausoleum * [[Wadō]], 1st year (708): {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}}, {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}} * Wadō, 2nd year (709): Passed away (Sangi, Shikibu-kyō, Taishōgun)
== References == * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟}}, ''Shoku Nihongi (Vol. 1),'' [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1992, ISBN 4061590308. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎}}, supervised by {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄}}, ''Dictionary of Japanese Ancient Clan Names,'' [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]], 1990, ISBN 4642022430. * {{ill|Takeuchi Risan|ja|竹内理三}}, ''Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Names (Vol. 4),'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1963, ISBN 4642020047. * [https://www.bgf.or.jp/bgmanga/?s=%E4%B8%8B%E6%AF%9B%E9%87%8E%E5%8F%A4%E9%BA%BB%E5%91%82 Free Manga: "Shimotsukeno no Komaro"], Published by the Shimotsuke City Board of Education, March 2022.

In April of the same year, Komaro and two others lectured on the new laws to the court officials.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 4th month, 7th day.</ref> By August, the Taihō Code was completed.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 8th month, 3rd day.</ref> In recognition of his contributions, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} in 702 and was later granted 30 ''kōden'' (fields for merit) and 50 ''fuko'' (households for support).<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 3rd year, 2nd month, 15th day and 3rd month, 7th day.</ref>
Under Emperor Monmu, Komaro served as {{ill|Hyōbu-kyō|ja|兵部卿}} while being promoted to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従四位上}} in 705.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin,'' Monmu 4th year, 4th month, 20th day.</ref> In 707, following the emperor’s death, Komaro was appointed to oversee the construction of the imperial mausoleum.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Keiun 4th year, 10th month, 3rd day.</ref> Additionally, he requested a name change for his relative {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}}, granting permission to adopt the surname {{ill|Shimotsukeno Kawachi|ja|下毛野川内朝臣}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Keiun 4th year, 3rd month, 22nd day.</ref>
During the [[Empress Genmei]]'s reign in 708, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}} and later {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}}. However, he passed away in December 709.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Wado 2nd year, 12th month, 20th day.</ref> Following his death, there were no Sangi appointments for eight years until {{ill|Fujiwara no Fusasaki|ja|ja|藤原房前}} assumed the role in 717.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin.''</ref>
== Legacy == In 2016, the history of Shimotsukeno no Komaro was adapted into a manga by the Oyamamachi North {{ill|Rotary Club|ja|ロータリークラブ}}.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, April 14, 2016.</ref> In 2017, the manga was converted into a video format.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, February 19, 2017.</ref>
== Official Titles == According to the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史}}: * [[Empress Jitō]], 3rd year (689): {{ill|Chokushishi|ja|直広肆}} * [[Emperor Monmu]], 4th year (700): {{ill|Chokushisan|ja|直広参}} * [[Taihō]], 1st year (701): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下}}, Right {{ill|Ōben|ja|大弁}} * Taihō, 2nd year (702): {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} * Taihō, 3rd year (703): Merit fields (10 ''kōden'' and 20 ''kōden'') * [[Keiun]], 2nd year (705): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従四位上}}, concurrently appointed {{ill|Hyōbu-kyō|ja|兵部卿}} * Keiun, 4th year (707): Overseer of the Imperial Mausoleum * [[Wadō]], 1st year (708): {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}}, {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}} * Wadō, 2nd year (709): Passed away (Sangi, Shikibu-kyō, Taishōgun)

'''Shimotsukeno no Komaro''' (下毛野 古麻呂) was a noble of the late [[Asuka period]]. His name is also recorded as '''Komaro''' (子麻呂). His [[kabane]] was initially ''Kimi'' and later ''Asomi''. He was the son of {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Kusimaro|ja|下毛野久志麻呂}}, who held the rank of {{ill|Daigi|ja|大義 (冠位)}}. Komaro’s final rank was {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|ja|正四位下}}, and he served as {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}.
== Origins == Shimotsukeno no Komaro belonged to the {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|ja|下毛野氏}}, which originated from the {{ill|Shimotsukeno Kokuzō||ja|下毛野国造}} lineage. His base of operations was in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja||ja|下野国}}, specifically in the {{ill|Kawachi District|ja||ja|河内郡}}. He is believed to have founded {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja||ja|下野薬師寺跡}}, which became the clan's temple.
== Career == In the third year of [[Empress Jitō]]’s reign (689), Komaro successfully petitioned to free 600 [[slaves]], an action approved by the court. At the time, he held the rank of {{ill|Chokushishi|ja|直広肆}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Empress Jitō, 3rd year, 10th month, 22nd day.</ref>
In the fourth year of [[Emperor Monmu]]'s reign (700), Komaro was appointed, along with {{ill|Prince Osakabe|ja|忍壁皇子}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|ja|藤原不比等}}, and {{ill|Awata no Mahito|ja|粟田真人}}, to draft the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}. At the time, he held the rank of {{ill|Chokushisan|ja|直広参}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Emperor Monmu, 4th year, 6th month, 17th day.</ref> The following year (701), after the enforcement of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, he was promoted to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下}}. In April of the same year, Komaro and two others lectured on the new laws to the court officials.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 4th month, 7th day.</ref> By August, the Taihō Code was completed.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 8th month, 3rd day.</ref> In recognition of his contributions, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} in 702 and was later granted 30 ''kōden'' (fields for merit) and 50 ''fuko'' (households for support).<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 3rd year, 2nd month, 15th day and 3rd month, 7th day.</ref>
Under Emperor Monmu, Komaro served as {{ill|Hyōbu-kyō|ja|兵部卿}} while being promoted to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従四位上}} in 705.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin,'' Monmu 4th year, 4th month, 20th day.</ref> In 707, following the emperor’s death, Komaro was appointed to oversee the construction of the imperial mausoleum.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Keiun 4th year, 10th month, 3rd day.</ref> Additionally, he requested a name change for his relative {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}}, granting permission to adopt the surname {{ill|Shimotsukeno Kawachi|ja|下毛野川内朝臣}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Keiun 4th year, 3rd month, 22nd day.</ref>
During the [[Empress Genmei]]'s reign in 708, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}} and later {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}}. However, he passed away in December 709.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Wado 2nd year, 12th month, 20th day.</ref> Following his death, there were no Sangi appointments for eight years until {{ill|Fujiwara no Fusasaki|ja|ja|藤原房前}} assumed the role in 717.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin.''</ref>
== Legacy == In 2016, the history of Shimotsukeno no Komaro was adapted into a manga by the Oyamamachi North {{ill|Rotary Club|ja|ロータリークラブ}}.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, April 14, 2016.</ref> In 2017, the manga was converted into a video format.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, February 19, 2017.</ref>
== Official Titles == According to the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史}}: * [[Empress Jitō]], 3rd year (689): {{ill|Chokushishi|ja|直広肆}} * [[Emperor Monmu]], 4th year (700): {{ill|Chokushisan|ja|直広参}} * [[Taihō]], 1st year (701): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下}}, Right {{ill|Ōben|ja|大弁}} * Taihō, 2nd year (702): {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} * Taihō, 3rd year (703): Merit fields (10 ''kōden'' and 20 ''kōden'') * [[Keiun]], 2nd year (705): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従四位上}}, concurrently appointed {{ill|Hyōbu-kyō|ja|兵部卿}} * Keiun, 4th year (707): Overseer of the Imperial Mausoleum * [[Wadō]], 1st year (708): {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}}, {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}} * Wadō, 2nd year (709): Passed away (Sangi, Shikibu-kyō, Taishōgun)

revision.comment: Text replacement - "従四位下}}" to "従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank}}"
revision.text:
'''Shimotsukeno no Komaro''' (下毛野 古麻呂) was a noble of the late [[Asuka period]]. His name is also recorded as '''Komaro''' (子麻呂). His [[kabane]] was initially ''Kimi'' and later ''Asomi''. He was the son of {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Kusimaro|ja|下毛野久志麻呂}}, who held the rank of {{ill|Daigi|ja|大義 (冠位)}}. Komaro’s final rank was {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|ja|正四位下}}, and he served as {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}.
== Origins == Shimotsukeno no Komaro belonged to the {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|ja|下毛野氏}}, which originated from the {{ill|Shimotsukeno Kokuzō||ja|下毛野国造}} lineage. His base of operations was in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja||ja|下野国}}, specifically in the {{ill|Kawachi District|ja||ja|河内郡}}. He is believed to have founded {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja||ja|下野薬師寺跡}}, which became the clan's temple.
== Career == In the third year of [[Empress Jitō]]’s reign (689), Komaro successfully petitioned to free 600 [[slaves]], an action approved by the court. At the time, he held the rank of {{ill|Chokushishi|ja|直広肆}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Empress Jitō, 3rd year, 10th month, 22nd day.</ref>
In the fourth year of [[Emperor Monmu]]'s reign (700), Komaro was appointed, along with {{ill|Prince Osakabe|ja|忍壁皇子}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|ja|藤原不比等}}, and {{ill|Awata no Mahito|ja|粟田真人}}, to draft the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}. At the time, he held the rank of {{ill|Chokushisan|ja|直広参}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Emperor Monmu, 4th year, 6th month, 17th day.</ref> The following year (701), after the enforcement of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, he was promoted to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank}}. In April of the same year, Komaro and two others lectured on the new laws to the court officials.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 4th month, 7th day.</ref> By August, the Taihō Code was completed.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 8th month, 3rd day.</ref> In recognition of his contributions, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} in 702 and was later granted 30 ''kōden'' (fields for merit) and 50 ''fuko'' (households for support).<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 3rd year, 2nd month, 15th day and 3rd month, 7th day.</ref>
Under Emperor Monmu, Komaro served as {{ill|Hyōbu-kyō|ja|兵部卿}} while being promoted to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従四位上}} in 705.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin,'' Monmu 4th year, 4th month, 20th day.</ref> In 707, following the emperor’s death, Komaro was appointed to oversee the construction of the imperial mausoleum.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Keiun 4th year, 10th month, 3rd day.</ref> Additionally, he requested a name change for his relative {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}}, granting permission to adopt the surname {{ill|Shimotsukeno Kawachi|ja|下毛野川内朝臣}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Keiun 4th year, 3rd month, 22nd day.</ref>
During the [[Empress Genmei]]'s reign in 708, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}} and later {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}}. However, he passed away in December 709.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Wado 2nd year, 12th month, 20th day.</ref> Following his death, there were no Sangi appointments for eight years until {{ill|Fujiwara no Fusasaki|ja|ja|藤原房前}} assumed the role in 717.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin.''</ref>
== Legacy == In 2016, the history of Shimotsukeno no Komaro was adapted into a manga by the Oyamamachi North {{ill|Rotary Club|ja|ロータリークラブ}}.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, April 14, 2016.</ref> In 2017, the manga was converted into a video format.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, February 19, 2017.</ref>
== Official Titles == According to the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史}}: * [[Empress Jitō]], 3rd year (689): {{ill|Chokushishi|ja|直広肆}} * [[Emperor Monmu]], 4th year (700): {{ill|Chokushisan|ja|直広参}} * [[Taihō]], 1st year (701): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank}}, Right {{ill|Ōben|ja|大弁}} * Taihō, 2nd year (702): {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} * Taihō, 3rd year (703): Merit fields (10 ''kōden'' and 20 ''kōden'') * [[Keiun]], 2nd year (705): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従四位上}}, concurrently appointed {{ill|Hyōbu-kyō|ja|兵部卿}} * Keiun, 4th year (707): Overseer of the Imperial Mausoleum * [[Wadō]], 1st year (708): {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}}, {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}} * Wadō, 2nd year (709): Passed away (Sangi, Shikibu-kyō, Taishōgun)
== References == * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟}}, ''Shoku Nihongi (Vol. 1),'' [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1992, ISBN 4061590308. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎}}, supervised by {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄}}, ''Dictionary of Japanese Ancient Clan Names,'' [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]], 1990, ISBN 4642022430. * {{ill|Takeuchi Risan|ja|竹内理三}}, ''Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Names (Vol. 4),'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1963, ISBN 4642020047. * [https://www.bgf.or.jp/bgmanga/?s=%E4%B8%8B%E6%AF%9B%E9%87%8E%E5%8F%A4%E9%BA%BB%E5%91%82 Free Manga: "Shimotsukeno no Komaro"], Published by the Shimotsuke City Board of Education, March 2022.

Komaro’s final rank was {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|ja|正四位下}}, and he served as {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}.
== Origins == Shimotsukeno no Komaro belonged to the {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|ja|下毛野氏|lt=Shimotsukeno clan}}, which originated from the {{ill|Shimotsukeno Kokuzō||ja|下毛野国造}} lineage. '''Shimotsukeno no Komaro''' (下毛野 古麻呂) was a noble of the late [[Asuka period]]. His name is also recorded as '''Komaro''' (子麻呂). His [[kabane]] was initially ''Kimi'' and later ''Asomi''. He was the son of {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Kusimaro|ja|下毛野久志麻呂}}, who held the rank of {{ill|Daigi|ja|大義 (冠位)}}. Komaro’s final rank was {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|ja|正四位下}}, and he served as {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}.
== Origins == Shimotsukeno no Komaro belonged to the {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|ja|下毛野氏|lt=Shimotsukeno clan}}, which originated from the {{ill|Shimotsukeno Kokuzō||ja|下毛野国造}} lineage. His base of operations was in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja||ja|下野国}}, specifically in the {{ill|Kawachi District|ja||ja|河内郡}}. He is believed to have founded {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja||ja|下野薬師寺跡}}, which became the clan's temple.
== Career == In the third year of [[Empress Jitō]]’s reign (689), Komaro successfully petitioned to free 600 [[slaves]], an action approved by the court. At the time, he held the rank of {{ill|Chokushishi|ja|直広肆}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Empress Jitō, 3rd year, 10th month, 22nd day.</ref>
In the fourth year of [[Emperor Monmu]]'s reign (700), Komaro was appointed, along with {{ill|Prince Osakabe|ja|忍壁皇子}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|ja|藤原不比等}}, and {{ill|Awata no Mahito|ja|粟田真人}}, to draft the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}. At the time, he held the rank of {{ill|Chokushisan|ja|直広参}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Emperor Monmu, 4th year, 6th month, 17th day.</ref> The following year (701), after the enforcement of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, he was promoted to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank}}. In April of the same year, Komaro and two others lectured on the new laws to the court officials.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 4th month, 7th day.</ref> By August, the Taihō Code was completed.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 8th month, 3rd day.</ref> In recognition of his contributions, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} in 702 and was later granted 30 ''kōden'' (fields for merit) and 50 ''fuko'' (households for support).<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 3rd year, 2nd month, 15th day and 3rd month, 7th day.</ref>
Under Emperor Monmu, Komaro served as {{ill|Hyōbu-kyō|ja|兵部卿}} while being promoted to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従四位上}} in 705.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin,'' Monmu 4th year, 4th month, 20th day.</ref> In 707, following the emperor’s death, Komaro was appointed to oversee the construction of the imperial mausoleum.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Keiun 4th year, 10th month, 3rd day.</ref> Additionally, he requested a name change for his relative {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}}, granting permission to adopt the surname {{ill|Shimotsukeno Kawachi|ja|下毛野川内朝臣}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Keiun 4th year, 3rd month, 22nd day.</ref>
During the [[Empress Genmei]]'s reign in 708, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}} and later {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}}. However, he passed away in December 709.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Wado 2nd year, 12th month, 20th day.</ref> Following his death, there were no Sangi appointments for eight years until {{ill|Fujiwara no Fusasaki|ja|ja|藤原房前}} assumed the role in 717.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin.''</ref>
== Legacy == In 2016, the history of Shimotsukeno no Komaro was adapted into a manga by the Oyamamachi North {{ill|Rotary Club|ja|ロータリークラブ}}.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, April 14, 2016.</ref> In 2017, the manga was converted into a video format.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, February 19, 2017.</ref>
== Official Titles == According to the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史}}: * [[Empress Jitō]], 3rd year (689): {{ill|Chokushishi|ja|直広肆}} * [[Emperor Monmu]], 4th year (700): {{ill|Chokushisan|ja|直広参}} * [[Taihō]], 1st year (701): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank}}, Right {{ill|Ōben|ja|大弁}} * Taihō, 2nd year (702): {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} * Taihō, 3rd year (703): Merit fields (10 ''kōden'' and 20 ''kōden'') * [[Keiun]], 2nd year (705): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従四位上}}, concurrently appointed {{ill|Hyōbu-kyō|ja|兵部卿}} * Keiun, 4th year (707): Overseer of the Imperial Mausoleum * [[Wadō]], 1st year (708): {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}}, {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}} * Wadō, 2nd year (709): Passed away (Sangi, Shikibu-kyō, Taishōgun)
== References == * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟}}, ''Shoku Nihongi (Vol. 1),'' [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1992, ISBN 4061590308. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎}}, supervised by {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄}}, ''Dictionary of Japanese Ancient Clan Names,'' [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]], 1990, ISBN 4642022430. * {{ill|Takeuchi Risan|ja|竹内理三}}, ''Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Names (Vol. 4),'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1963, ISBN 4642020047. * [https://www.bgf.or.jp/bgmanga/?s=%E4%B8%8B%E6%AF%9B%E9%87%8E%E5%8F%A4%E9%BA%BB%E5%91%82 Free Manga: "Shimotsukeno no Komaro"], Published by the Shimotsuke City Board of Education, March 2022.

'''Shimotsukeno no Komaro''' (下毛野 古麻呂) was a noble of the late [[Asuka period]]. His name is also recorded as '''Komaro''' (子麻呂). His [[kabane]] was initially ''Kimi'' and later ''Asomi''. He was the son of {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Kusimaro|ja|下毛野久志麻呂}}, who held the rank of {{ill|Daigi|ja|大義 (冠位)}}. Komaro’s final rank was {{ill|Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|ja|正四位下}}, and he served as {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}}.
== Origins == Shimotsukeno no Komaro belonged to the {{ill|Shimotsukeno clan|ja|ja|下毛野氏|lt=Shimotsukeno clan}}, which originated from the {{ill|Shimotsukeno Kokuzō||ja|下毛野国造}} lineage. His base of operations was in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja||ja|下野国}}, specifically in the {{ill|Kawachi District|ja||ja|河内郡}}. He is believed to have founded {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja||ja|下野薬師寺跡}}, which became the clan's temple.
== Career == In the third year of [[Empress Jitō]]’s reign (689), Komaro successfully petitioned to free 600 [[slaves]], an action approved by the court. At the time, he held the rank of {{ill|Chokushishi|ja|直広肆}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Empress Jitō, 3rd year, 10th month, 22nd day.</ref>
In the fourth year of [[Emperor Monmu]]'s reign (700), Komaro was appointed, along with {{ill|Prince Osakabe|ja|忍壁皇子}}, {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|ja|藤原不比等}}, and {{ill|Awata no Mahito|ja|粟田真人}}, to draft the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}. At the time, he held the rank of {{ill|Chokushisan|ja|直広参}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Emperor Monmu, 4th year, 6th month, 17th day.</ref> The following year (701), after the enforcement of the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}}, he was promoted to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank}}. In April of the same year, Komaro and two others lectured on the new laws to the court officials.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 4th month, 7th day.</ref> By August, the Taihō Code was completed.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 1st year, 8th month, 3rd day.</ref> In recognition of his contributions, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} in 702 and was later granted 30 ''kōden'' (fields for merit) and 50 ''fuko'' (households for support).<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Taihō 3rd year, 2nd month, 15th day and 3rd month, 7th day.</ref>
Under Emperor Monmu, Komaro served as {{ill|Hyōbu-kyō|ja|兵部卿}} while being promoted to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従四位上}} in 705.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin,'' Monmu 4th year, 4th month, 20th day.</ref> In 707, following the emperor’s death, Komaro was appointed to oversee the construction of the imperial mausoleum.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Keiun 4th year, 10th month, 3rd day.</ref> Additionally, he requested a name change for his relative {{ill|Shimotsukeno no Ishishiro|ja|下毛野石代}}, granting permission to adopt the surname {{ill|Shimotsukeno Kawachi|ja|下毛野川内朝臣}}.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Keiun 4th year, 3rd month, 22nd day.</ref>
During the [[Empress Genmei]]'s reign in 708, Komaro was appointed {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}} and later {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}}. However, he passed away in December 709.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi,'' Wado 2nd year, 12th month, 20th day.</ref> Following his death, there were no Sangi appointments for eight years until {{ill|Fujiwara no Fusasaki|ja|ja|藤原房前}} assumed the role in 717.<ref>''Kugyō Bunin.''</ref>
== Legacy == In 2016, the history of Shimotsukeno no Komaro was adapted into a manga by the Oyamamachi North {{ill|Rotary Club|ja|ロータリークラブ}}.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, April 14, 2016.</ref> In 2017, the manga was converted into a video format.<ref>''Yomiuri Shimbun,'' Tochigi edition, February 19, 2017.</ref>
== Official Titles == According to the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史}}: * [[Empress Jitō]], 3rd year (689): {{ill|Chokushishi|ja|直広肆}} * [[Emperor Monmu]], 4th year (700): {{ill|Chokushisan|ja|直広参}} * [[Taihō]], 1st year (701): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank}}, Right {{ill|Ōben|ja|大弁}} * Taihō, 2nd year (702): {{ill|Sangi|ja|参議}} * Taihō, 3rd year (703): Merit fields (10 ''kōden'' and 20 ''kōden'') * [[Keiun]], 2nd year (705): {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|ja|従四位上}}, concurrently appointed {{ill|Hyōbu-kyō|ja|兵部卿}} * Keiun, 4th year (707): Overseer of the Imperial Mausoleum * [[Wadō]], 1st year (708): {{ill|Shikibu-kyō|ja|式部卿}}, {{ill|Taishōgun|ja|大将軍}} * Wadō, 2nd year (709): Passed away (Sangi, Shikibu-kyō, Taishōgun)
== References == * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟}}, ''Shoku Nihongi (Vol. 1),'' [[Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko]], 1992, ISBN 4061590308. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎}}, supervised by {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄}}, ''Dictionary of Japanese Ancient Clan Names,'' [[Yoshikawa Kōbunkan]], 1990, ISBN 4642022430. * {{ill|Takeuchi Risan|ja|竹内理三}}, ''Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Names (Vol. 4),'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1963, ISBN 4642020047. * [https://www.bgf.or.jp/bgmanga/?s=%E4%B8%8B%E6%AF%9B%E9%87%8E%E5%8F%A4%E9%BA%BB%E5%91%82 Free Manga: "Shimotsukeno no Komaro"], Published by the Shimotsuke City Board of Education, March 2022.

His base of operations was in {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|ja||ja|下野国}}, specifically in the {{ill|Kawachi District|ja||ja|河内郡}}. He is believed to have founded {{ill|Shimotsuke Yakushiji|ja||ja|下野薬師寺跡}}, which became the clan's temple.
